claims.claim_text: OpenTelemetry provides a common API and SDK, OTLP protocol, and Collector for telemetry collection, processing, and export.
claims.rationale: The official Collector documentation describes receivers, processors, exporters, pipelines, and OTLP-based telemetry flow.

claims.claim_text: The Collector is not a full observability backend and does not replace dashboards, storage, alerting, or incident workflows.
claims.rationale: OpenTelemetry's Collector docs define the Collector as a telemetry collection and processing component rather than a storage or alerting backend.

claims.claim_text: Java auto-instrumentation can instrument Spring, JDBC, and HTTP clients without application code changes.
claims.span_hint: section: Instrumentation Strategy: Automatic First, Manual Where It Matters
claims.rationale: The official Java agent supported-libraries page lists supported Spring, JDBC, and HTTP client instrumentations.

claims.claim_text: Python auto-instrumentation can instrument many common frameworks and client libraries without changing application code.
claims.span_hint: section: Instrumentation Strategy: Automatic First, Manual Where It Matters
claims.rationale: The official Python zero-code instrumentation docs describe automatic instrumentation for supported libraries.

claims.claim_text: Missing service.name may cause telemetry to appear under an unknown service or be grouped with unrelated telemetry.
claims.span_hint: section: Instrumentation Strategy: Automatic First, Manual Where It Matters
claims.rationale: The semantic conventions registry defines service.name and the unknown_service fallback behavior.

claims.claim_text: In Prometheus, concrete path labels such as /orders/123 and /orders/124 create separate time series.
claims.span_hint: section: Instrumentation Strategy: Automatic First, Manual Where It Matters
claims.rationale: Prometheus documents that a time series is identified by metric name plus label set, so changing label values creates distinct series.

claims.claim_text: OpenTelemetry includes B3 propagation support for compatibility with Zipkin-era services.
claims.span_hint: section: Context Propagation: The Difference Between Spans and Traces
claims.rationale: The OpenTelemetry propagators API documentation includes B3 among the distributed propagators.

claims.claim_text: Collector receivers accept data, processors modify or decide what to keep, exporters send data onward, and pipelines connect those pieces per signal type.
claims.span_hint: section: The Collector: Receivers, Processors, Exporters, and Pipelines
claims.rationale: The official Collector docs define receivers, processors, exporters, and pipelines.

claims.claim_text: The Collector can receive OTLP, scrape Prometheus endpoints, accept Zipkin traffic, batch data, apply memory limits, filter spans, and export to multiple destinations.
claims.span_hint: section: The Collector: Receivers, Processors, Exporters, and Pipelines
claims.rationale: Collector documentation and component pages describe these receiver, processor, and exporter capabilities.

claims.claim_text: OpenTelemetry Collector can be deployed as an agent, gateway, sidecar, or standalone process.
claims.rationale: The official deployment documentation lists Collector deployment patterns including agent, gateway, sidecar, and standalone.

claims.claim_text: Tail sampling waits until a trace is complete or until a decision timeout is reached, and can keep errors, slow traces, and a percentage of normal traces.
claims.span_hint: section: Sampling, Filtering, and Cost Control
claims.rationale: The OpenTelemetry sampling docs explain head and tail sampling behavior and decision criteria.

claims.claim_text: Tail-sampling gateways need all spans for the same trace or trace-aware routing by trace ID.
claims.span_hint: section: Sampling, Filtering, and Cost Control
claims.rationale: The gateway deployment documentation discusses routing spans for the same trace to the same Collector for tail sampling.

claims.claim_text: Collector self-metrics such as accepted spans, refused spans, processor drops, exporter queue size, and send failures help distinguish upstream from downstream problems.
claims.rationale: The internal telemetry docs enumerate Collector metrics for receivers, processors, exporter queues, and send failures.

claims.claim_text: OTLP/gRPC commonly uses port 4317 and OTLP/HTTP commonly uses port 4318.
claims.rationale: The OTLP specification documents default ports and transport behavior for OTLP/gRPC and OTLP/HTTP.

claims.claim_text: OpenTelemetry was created by merging OpenTracing and OpenCensus.
claims.rationale: The official overview states that OpenTelemetry is the result of merging OpenTracing and OpenCensus.

claims.claim_text: Metrics labels with raw user IDs, order IDs, request IDs, or full URLs can create high-cardinality series and increase cost or query latency.
claims.span_hint: sections: Instrumentation Strategy; Sampling, Filtering, and Cost Control; Common Mistakes
claims.rationale: Prometheus's data model explains that each unique label set creates a distinct time series, which supports the high-cardinality warning.

further_reading.title: OpenTelemetry Traces
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains spans, traces, parent-child relationships, and the tracing model used throughout the module.

further_reading.title: OpenTelemetry Collector
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative overview of Collector receivers, processors, exporters, and pipelines.

further_reading.title: OTLP Specification
further_reading.why_relevant: Defines OTLP transports, default ports, and protocol behavior needed for debugging exporter and receiver mismatches.

further_reading.title: OpenTelemetry Sampling
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers head and tail sampling trade-offs that drive the module's cost and incident-response discussion.

notes: No war stories, incident timelines, dated pricing, CVEs, legal claims, or hard statistics were found. Code blocks and exercises were ignored except where surrounding prose made a factual protocol or capability claim.
